text.title: Browser Automation Overview Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the browser-automation-overview methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: If the project language is Python or .NET, choose Playwright. Puppeteer is JS/TS-only.
rule.rationale: Puppeteer Python bindings (pyppeteer) are unmaintained; Playwright has first-class language bindings.

rule.statement: If the workload requires WebKit/Safari or Firefox testing, choose Playwright.
rule.rationale: Puppeteer targets Chromium only; cross-browser parity is Playwright's headline feature.

rule.statement: If the team has limited bandwidth for handling flakes, choose Playwright for its auto-wait and trace viewer.
rule.rationale: Auto-wait reduces sleep-debugging; trace viewer turns CI flakes into reproducible recordings.

rule.statement: If the target does not require JavaScript rendering, skip both — use httpx/requests/curl.
rule.rationale: Spinning up a browser for a static endpoint wastes CPU and memory.

rule.statement: After picking a tool, immediately load the matching sibling methodology (puppeteer-launch-setup or playwright-automation or web-scraping-resilience).
rule.rationale: This file is the decision layer only; implementation patterns live in siblings.

rule.statement: If the tool is already chosen, or the workload is pure HTTP, or it is a one-time export script, skip this methodology.
rule.rationale: Routing only adds value when the choice is open.
